Blockchain for Supply Chain: Uses and Benefits
==============================================

Michael Chen | Content Strategist | August 8, 2024

![Image 2](https://www.oracle.com/a/ocom/img/rc24-bsa-blockchain-for-supply-chain.jpg)

**In This Article**

*   [What Is Blockchain in Supply Chain Management?](https://www.oracle.com/blockchain/what-is-blockchain/blockchain-for-supply-chain/#what-is)

*   [Why Use Blockchain for Supply Chain Management?](https://www.oracle.com/blockchain/what-is-blockchain/blockchain-for-supply-chain/#why-use)
*   [Blockchain Capabilities in Supply Chains](https://www.oracle.com/blockchain/what-is-blockchain/blockchain-for-supply-chain/#capabilities)
*   [How Is Blockchain Used in Supply Chain Management?](https://www.oracle.com/blockchain/what-is-blockchain/blockchain-for-supply-chain/#how-is-it-used)
*   [Blockchain for Supply Chain Benefits and Challenges](https://www.oracle.com/blockchain/what-is-blockchain/blockchain-for-supply-chain/#benefits-and-challenges)
*   [Integration with Emerging Technologies](https://www.oracle.com/blockchain/what-is-blockchain/blockchain-for-supply-chain/#integration)
*   [Blockchain in Supply Chains: Case Study](https://www.oracle.com/blockchain/what-is-blockchain/blockchain-for-supply-chain/#case-study)
*   [Blockchain Supply Chain FAQs](https://www.oracle.com/blockchain/what-is-blockchain/blockchain-for-supply-chain/#faqs)

Was that diamond mined ethically? Has a vaccine been kept cold on its journey from manufacturer to rural clinic? Should this batch of lettuce be part of a recall over possible contamination? As goods from mined minerals to sushi-grade tuna to pharmaceuticals move through complex supply chains, blockchain technology creates a transparent and trustworthy record of each transaction, from source to consumer.

Blockchain is a decentralized, distributed ledger technology that securely records transactions—blocks—using multiple computers, which comprise the chain. Once a block is verified, it’s added to the chain, creating a transparent and unchangeable record of all transactions. While blockchain technology is entrenched in cryptocurrency, new and exciting use cases are emerging in retail, finance, healthcare, and other sectors. There’s one common thread: These industries need robust supply chain management that delivers secure transactions and on-demand insights.

There’s a human element, too, because while blockchain helps enable strong traceability, it’s just a piece of the puzzle. Organizations need processes to dependably label individual goods or components so they can be traced back to lots, for example. Still, blockchain provides the foundation for a system where everyone involved can track exactly what happened at each step. Now, if a product needs to be recalled, affected items can be identified quickly. The tamperproof nature of blockchain helps companies demonstrate compliance with regulations or safety standards, and buyers get a clear picture of where goods are as they progress through the supply chain.

What Is Blockchain in Supply Chain Management?
----------------------------------------------

Blockchain in supply chain management uses blockchain’s decentralized, trusted, distributed ledger technology to provide transparency and traceability of transactions within a supply chain. Typically, a permissioned blockchain is used in a supply chain, meaning that users must be members of the relevant blockchain consortium or invited by a member. Each transaction is recorded within a block in the chain, creating a secure and trustworthy record of exchanges between parties. Once written, transactions can’t be changed. Any attempt to alter records will be discovered since multiple copies of the transaction are recorded. Blockchain use reduces fraud and increases efficiency by providing real-time visibility of goods as they move through the supply chain.

For supply chain management, blockchain can be an ideal tool and provide additional valuable benefits, including the following:

*   The ability to verify the authenticity of products, track items from origin to destination, and ensure compliance with controls such as storage temperature and humidity.
*   Validated data that can be audited by multiple parties means fraudulent updates or errors are reliably caught.
*   Smart contracts automatically execute payments at agreed-on milestones, such as delivery of items in good condition, helping with cash flow.

One interesting use case is tokenized assets, where digital tokens are combined with a blockchain to authenticate ownership of physical or digital assets, such as gems, real estate, and intellectual property. Each token acts as a digital certificate linked to a specific portion of the underlying asset.

Companies are also using blockchain-connected scanners that can read barcodes, RFID tags, or other identifiers just like a standard scanner. But instead of simply storing data, scanned information is transmitted to a blockchain for precise tracking of an item’s journey through the supply chain.

**Key Takeaways**

*   Blockchain creates and maintains a digital ledger where new records can be added in cleartext or securely using an irreversible encryption technique called one-way hashing. Blockchain ledgers may be audited to ensure authenticity.
*   Supply chain security benefits from blockchain’s shared access to authoritative data. This builds trust and helps prevent fraud.
*   Blockchain can already integrate with other technologies in use throughout supply chains, including scanners and Internet of Things (IoT) devices. Potential innovations include integrating blockchain with technologies such as digital identity authentication.
*   Integrating a blockchain comes with human challenges, including putting labeling processes in place and getting all stakeholders to adopt the technology uniformly.

Blockchain for Supply Chains Explained
--------------------------------------

Before diving into the specifics of blockchain for supply chains, it’s important to understand [how blockchain works](https://www.oracle.com/blockchain/what-is-blockchain/). Simply put, a blockchain consists of blocks of records, linked together and stored in multiple locations to create an unalterable ledger that’s readable by authorized parties. Attempts to tamper with records are easily detected, so data stored in a blockchain can be considered truthful.

With that in mind, consider how useful blockchain might be in your supply chain. For example, procurement managers often go through many steps while tracking goods and services. These processes involve multiple parties, usually at multiple locations. If they all update a blockchain as they perform their parts of the process, transparency is an inherent benefit.

And the accuracy and security of blockchain means every party will be on the same page and relying on the same information. This helps minimize disruptions and errors, and if something does go wrong it’s easy to discover when and where the problem occurred.

Why Use Blockchain for Supply Chain Management?
-----------------------------------------------

In many ways, blockchain is an ideal fit for supply chain management. Blockchain technology allows for a tamperproof and decentralized record of transactions, making it easier to track and verify the condition of goods as they move through the supply chain. Blockchain technology brings inherent security and traceability, all while providing a data source that’s readable by all parties. Common supply chain issues, including late updates, miscommunication, or faulty authentication, are minimized.

Blockchain can help prevent fraud and errors, reduce paperwork, improve cash flow with automated payments as milestones are met, and overall provide a reliable system for tracking goods from production to delivery. That makes it a worthwhile investment in an organization’s operational infrastructure.

Blockchain Capabilities in Supply Chains
----------------------------------------

For a supply chain, blockchains provide transparency, security, traceability, and visibility. These deliver both direct and indirect benefits by minimizing risk and connecting third-party stakeholders in the supply chain process.

Key capabilities include the following:

### Enhanced Traceability

Blockchain’s public ledger provides a product’s provenance, tracking it from start to finish. With a single ledger recording transactions for all stakeholders, a blockchain maximizes visibility by creating transparent updates at each stage of the supply chain. This enhanced traceability ensures each record retrieved is unaltered, while also providing up-to-the-minute notice of delays, damage, or other issues that require quick recovery plans.

### Smart Contracts in Supply Chains

Because the blockchain’s ledger shows all stakeholders when each step occurred, it can integrate with smart contracts that automatically execute actions called for in the contract when certain terms and conditions are met. Using blockchain in conjunction with smart contracts reduces disputes about milestones and contract terms being executed.

### Supply Chain Security

A blockchain addresses security by using one-way hashing and recording transactions on multiple systems; this ensures that data is, for all practical purposes, unchangeable. One-way hashing creates a unique signature that’s stored with data in the public ledger. If the data is changed, the signature won’t match when the hash is recalculated, and the owners of the blockchain will be notified. If both the hash and the data are changed, then copies of the ledger won’t agree with one another. At least three public ledgers are kept, so altering one will flag a problem and two good entries will still be intact. Altering every public ledger is a very difficult task.

Furthermore, timestamps are baked into the hash and linked to the previous entry in the blockchain, again making it practically impossible to falsify ledger entries. All of this makes for optimized authentication, which limits opportunities for real-world security risks, such as supply theft and substitution of counterfeit items.

How Is Blockchain Used in Supply Chain Management?
--------------------------------------------------

Blockchain can be a game-changing tool in supply chain management. It provides transparency, accountability, and an irrefutable record of transactions as goods and services move along the supply chain. For example, a contract might release payment to a shipping company upon confirmation of delivery at the designated location.

Prime examples of how different industries use blockchain for their supply chain management include the following:

### Logistics and Shipping

Blockchain provides a transparent and secure way to follow goods from initial packing to loading to movement through various transportation hubs, vehicles, and warehouses—that level of end-to-end tracking is particularly important and often difficult for global stakeholders.

In addition, for sensitive materials, such as medications, blockchain allows for faster, more secure monitoring compared with traditional processes. You can ensure items were kept at a constant temperature and not substituted with counterfeit goods. This is why companies including FedEx and UPS are exploring blockchain to expand their offerings and enhance transparency in shipping and delivery processes.

### Healthcare

The healthcare industry comes with unique supply chain management challenges, including control of regulated materials, such as prescription drugs, and special shipping requirements, such as refrigeration. Blockchain offers a fast and secure way to verify these requirements are met from production through to the consumer. Medical devices and materials that originate all over the world can use blockchain to unify reporting on extremely complex shipping processes and compliance with various regional laws and regulations.

### Manufacturing

Manufacturing processes often come with complex supply chains, sometimes with unavoidable single points of failure. Getting suppliers to use blockchain to verify and track the movement of goods can help manage that complexity. By its nature, blockchain provides the visibility and traceability that conventional tracking systems lack, limiting the diversion of materials and components, reducing counterfeits, and helping ensure compliance with standards. And using smart contracts that execute payments as milestones are met can give stakeholders a strong incentive to participate.

Blockchain for Supply Chain Benefits and Challenges
---------------------------------------------------

Blockchain represents a major change in how supply chains work. Because blockchain records transactions onto a single ledger, for example, it closes the visibility and accountability gaps that naturally arise with ad hoc supply chain management systems. At the same time, adding a blockchain can be a resource-intensive commitment because it requires that all participants modify their existing systems and processes.

The following are some of the most common benefits and attendant challenges of adding blockchain to a supply chain:

**A single, publicly readable transaction ledger.** Current supply chain management systems may rely on dated technologies and processes that exacerbate lag time, human error, and other issues because they often lack standards for data exchange. Blockchain provides an agreed-on format and mechanism that lets all parties see and update the most recent information possible once they adopt the technology.

**Verified transactions.** In blockchain for supply chain management, there’s no need to use third parties, such as financial institutions, to verify transactions. Because an encrypted and publicly accessible ledger exists, transactions are secure and visible to every participant in the supply chain—again, as long as they use the technology.

**Traceability with permanence.** Blockchain ledgers are permanent and unchangeable, so any issues within the supply chain can be easily traced back to their origin. Companies can know exactly which lots of food or medical items need to be recalled, saving money. Questions about the provenance of any item can be answered authoritatively.

**Supply chain accuracy.** Blockchain provides a better way to record documents. However, the participants or a third-party standards group must determine how documents are formatted and what information they include. To date, no common standards exist for supply chain management. Where blockchain is presently used, the initiative is often driven by very large companies that set up rules, including document formatting and content. Accuracy will be only as good as the specifics of what documents must show.

**Quick updates.** When the work is done to set it up properly, it’s inexpensive and easy to add documents to the blockchain ledger. That encourages quick and highly visible updates that show the status toward achieving goals on the way to fulfilling more significant milestones.

Integration with Emerging Technologies
--------------------------------------

Not that long ago, blockchain was the hot new tech trend, underpinning IT industry innovations like cryptocurrency and NFTs. Now that blockchain has become more established, the next step in its evolution is integration with other technologies that are helping organizations improve their operations. Fortunately, AI, IoT, and other technologies offer capabilities that complement blockchain. By combining them, supply chains will benefit from better and more automated workflow documentation.

### Blockchain and IoT Integration

[IoT devices](https://www.oracle.com/internet-of-things/) generate massive amounts of data. Think about, for example, IoT sensors on containers. Here’s how blockchain integration might work: A container with, say, fresh dairy has sensors that continually take temperature readings and track how long items have been in transit. The collected data is securely transmitted to a blockchain and stored on a distributed ledger across multiple nodes, ensuring immutability. Authorized participants in the blockchain can track the shipment with confidence. Meanwhile, smart contracts on the blockchain could be triggered by data events from IoT devices, automating actions or transactions, such as payment to a trucking company for getting fresh milk from dairy to plant quickly. This helps ensure product quality.

### AI with Blockchain in Supply Chains

For more complex supply chains, AI excels at analyzing vast amounts of collected data to identify patterns; predict problems, such as equipment failure; and optimize processes based on real-time information. By combining [AI’s predictive capabilities](https://www.oracle.com/artificial-intelligence/what-is-ai/) with blockchain’s secure data sharing, companies can improve the entire supply chain. Take our dairy delivery. AI could correlate data, such as the last time the cooling system was serviced along with a forecasted spike in temperature, to recommend using a different delivery vehicle. In addition, blockchain’s decentralized nature means it scales well, so it can provide timely data for analysis and for AI systems to use to trigger actions as milestones are met, such as sending notifications for delays or executing terms of smart contracts.

### Blockchain and Future Technologies in Supply Chains

As it stands, blockchain offers new opportunities to enhance technologies like AI and IoT in [supply chain management](https://www.oracle.com/scm/what-is-supply-chain-management/). New uses, such as the tokenization of goods, can create even faster transactions and greater transparency through digital representations of physical assets. Blockchain can empower consumers to learn about the products they purchase. Imagine enabling an end customer to scan a QR code on a product to access a detailed history on the blockchain, revealing the origin of materials and the carbon footprint of shipping, among other information.

Blockchain is already used to track currencies. For trading partners, it could facilitate secure peer-to-peer transactions, letting companies access financing on a decentralized platform and opening new expansion opportunities for smaller businesses.

Blockchain in Supply Chains: Case Study
---------------------------------------

[Consider Tracifier](https://www.oracle.com/customers/tracifier/), a German startup providing supply chain traceability solutions. Tracifier’s offerings include verification and tracking across the supply chain. In particular, the company sought to make it easier to track, trace, and verify food products—where they originated, when they were produced, and the relevant safety standards and inspections.

Tracifier saw a path to this through blockchain technology and integrated [Oracle Blockchain](https://www.oracle.com/blockchain/) into its supply chain management database. The company then launched a transparent and immutable digital ledger that authenticated the validity of transactions and records. For customers, the blockchain ensures reliable and accessible data for every step. Smart contracts, process automation, and greater transparency helped Tracifier’s customers reduce food processing costs by up to 40%. In addition, Tracifier saw its own operational improvements with greater efficiency, fewer delays, and reduced costs.

Oracle has innovative tools to help your organization integrate blockchain and, like Tracifier, make the most out of your supply chain management solution. Find out how Oracle can support bringing [blockchain into your supply chain process](https://www.oracle.com/scm/track-and-trace/).

Blockchain technology is evolving, and its potential applications in supply chain management are constantly expanding. As blockchain technology matures and integrates further with AI, IoT, and other innovations, we can expect even more groundbreaking applications to emerge and transform the future of supply chain management.

Blockchain Supply Chain FAQs
----------------------------

**What is blockchain and how does it apply to supply chains?**

A blockchain is a secure and decentralized digital ledger that’s publicly readable for auditing and other purposes. Its use of cryptography makes entries in the ledger unchangeable. For supply chains, that means records for logistics, inventory, and delivery can be continuously connected and updated, keeping all stakeholders informed and current.

**How can blockchain improve supply chains?**

Because supply chains involve many parties, often across the globe, an accurate, rapidly updated, secure, visible record benefits everyone involved. When blockchain is used, the single ledger consolidates everyone’s data into unchangeable records. That means when an update occurs, it pushes into the next stage with global visibility.

**How can blockchain enhance transparency and traceability in supply chains?**

Blockchain offers several key features that significantly enhance transparency and traceability in supply chains. The core feature is an immutable, or unalterable, ledger where transactions throughout the supply chain are recorded and shared. This ledger creates a verifiable record of every step a product takes, from raw materials to the final consumer. Everyone involved can access this record, ensuring everyone has the same information.

**How does blockchain handle data privacy and security, especially in a decentralized system?**

Blockchain’s use of one-way encryption and multiple copies of the ledger make it nearly impossible to create fraudulent entries or to change existing entries, helping ensure security. However, the technology’s intrinsic transparency can raise privacy issues, especially when public blockchains are used. Two solutions are the use of private blockchains that restrict access to authorized participants and hashing technology that converts sensitive data into a unique code (hash) before storing it on the blockchain. This protects the original data while still allowing verification of authenticity.

# Why and How to Employ Blockchain in Supply Chain Management (Tips & Success Stories)

![facebook](/img/svg/socials/facebook.svg)
![linkedin](/img/svg/socials/linkedin.svg)
![twitter](/img/svg/socials/tweeter.svg)

![blockchain and supply chain management best implementation practices](/img/articles/why-and-how-to-employ-blockchain-in-supply-chain-management-tips-and-success-stories/img01.jpg "By implementing blockchain for supply chain management, businesses can ensure secure and automated transactions, potentially saving operational")

**Key Takeaways**

Blockchain supply chain software enhances transparency and reduces costs by eliminating intermediaries.

In 2024, 47% of Inspectorio survey respondents identified blockchain as a crucial tech advancement to embrace for visibility and automation.

By applying blockchain in supply chain management, businesses enable secure audit trails, ensuring trustworthy logistics synchronization, real-time shipment monitoring, and automated settlements.

The blockchain supply chain market, valued at $3.25B in 2025, is expected to grow at a 59.8% CAGR, reaching $21.29B by 2029.

Since logistics has become a highly automated and digitized sphere, each of the many moving parts requires comprehensive oversight. As goods move from one location to another, it can be challenging to track all the transactions accurately within outdated [supply chain systems](/blog/supply-chain-management-software-system-its-features-benefits-and-how-to-integrate-a-suitable-one/).

This is where blockchain and supply chain management meet to ensure transparency — the key success metric. Considered one of the most impactful [logistics tech trends](/blog/top-logistics-technology-trends/), blockchain technology doesn’t only validate and store digital transactions; it protects systems from fraudulent activities. In fact, [47%](https://2325471.fs1.hubspotusercontent-na1.net/hubfs/2325471/State%20of%20Supply%20Chain%202024/20240522-PL-RP-StateofSupplyChain2024.pdf) of companies view blockchain as vital for visibility.

With 10+ years of automating and strengthening logistics operations with AI/[ML](/blog/adopting-machine-learning-in-supply-chain-and-logistics-for-successful-automation/), [IoT](/blog/embracing-iot-in-transportation-and-logistics-to-boost-supply-chain-visibility/), and blockchain-based tools, Acropolium has gained strategic insights for advanced tech implementations.

Today, we will explore how blockchain in SCM can enhance transparency, traceability, and efficiency with use cases, success cases, and best practices.

## What is Blockchain in Supply Chain Operations?

![statistics and projections of blockchain in supply chain management](/img/articles/why-and-how-to-employ-blockchain-in-supply-chain-management-tips-and-success-stories/img02.jpg "The blockchain supply chain market is seeing rapid growth and is estimated to reach $21.29 Billion in 2029.")

![statistics and projections of blockchain in supply chain management](/img/articles/why-and-how-to-employ-blockchain-in-supply-chain-management-tips-and-success-stories/img02.jpg "The blockchain supply chain market is seeing rapid growth and is estimated to reach $21.29 Billion in 2029.")

Blockchain is a decentralized ledger for storing and recording transactions securely, initially introduced for Bitcoin in 2008. It links transaction blocks in a chronological, immutable chain, verified by multiple participants without central control.

The combined power of blockchain and supply chain management lies in the secure operations of all logistics parties on a shared, usually [cloud-based](/blog/cloud-computing-in-logistics-and-supply-chain/) platform. This technology ensures a trusted, tamper-resistant audit trail, allowing for synchronized logistics, shipment monitoring, and automated settlements with minimal system changes.

Thus, anyone from manufacturers and shippers to carriers and brokers can maintain comprehensive records and prevent data breaches. And, with the huge impact on core industry activities, the blockchain supply chain market is [forecasted](https://www.thebusinessresearchcompany.com/report/blockchain-supply-chain-global-market-report) to grow impressively. Valued at $3.27B in 2025, it is anticipated to expand at a CAGR of 59.8% and reach $21.29B by 2029.

## Why Operate with Blockchain in the Supply Chain?

![benefits of blockchain in SCM](/img/articles/why-and-how-to-employ-blockchain-in-supply-chain-management-tips-and-success-stories/img03.jpg "When supply chain management and blockchain technology meet in logistics operations, they help businesses enhance transparency, improve traceability, and strenghten transactional security.")

![benefits of blockchain in SCM](/img/articles/why-and-how-to-employ-blockchain-in-supply-chain-management-tips-and-success-stories/img03.jpg "When supply chain management and blockchain technology meet in logistics operations, they help businesses enhance transparency, improve traceability, and strenghten transactional security.")

In 2024, [43.5%](https://content.beacon.com/hubfs/Guides/Supply%20Chain%20Visibility%20Capabilities%20Report%202024.pdf) of companies faced challenges in sharing information with logistics partners, emphasizing the need for better supply chain visibility. Indeed, the transparency brought by blockchain supply chain software can cut costs by eliminating intermediaries. At the same time, it reduces administrative overheads from development to delivery and returns.

Cost savings, however, are not the only ways you can benefit from blockchain technology.

### Enhanced Transparency

By integrating blockchain through smart sensors and RFID tags, companies can accurately track goods through the supply chain. Such IoT and blockchain-powered tools record conditions such as temperature and humidity at each stage.

With blockchain in SCM, transactions are time-stamped and up-to-date, sending the data to [logistics management systems](/blog/why-a-logistics-management-system-is-a-must-for-successful-supply-chain-operations/) and [supply chain analytics software](/blog/employing-supply-chain-analytics-software-for-efficient-workflows-key-features-use-cases/) in real time.

### Improved Traceability

In supply chain management, traceability refers to tracking inventory locations and custody from raw materials to end customers. Blockchain enables efficient live tracking with decentralized ledgers. Often found in [multi-carrier software](/blog/multi-carrier-shipping-software-a-guide-to-enhancing-shipping-efficiency/), the technology allows stakeholders to access detailed product information such as dates, prices, origin, quality, and certification.

### Increased Efficiency

Blockchain technology in supply chain management also contributes to timely resolved compliance problems and automated workflows, resulting in higher efficiency. The enhanced visibility helps detect and address issues like compliance violations, counterfeits, delays, and waste more rapidly.

In fact, the [Beacon’s report](https://content.beacon.com/hubfs/Guides/Supply%20Chain%20Visibility%20Capabilities%20Report%202024.pdf) says that blockchain is set out to be a game-changer in adherence to regulatory compliance within supply chains.

### Stronger Security

The cryptographic features protect sensitive information, making it harder to access or alter data for unauthorized entities. Supply chain and blockchain transparency allows all stakeholders to verify transactions independently, lowering the risk of compromise and errors.

Smart contracts automate processes, enable immediate invoice payments, and establish a transparent payment system, thereby reducing human error and fraud.

## Use Cases and Applications of Blockchain in Supply Chains

![blockchain supply chain use cases and applications](/img/articles/why-and-how-to-employ-blockchain-in-supply-chain-management-tips-and-success-stories/img04.jpg "Discover the globally adopted blockchain supply chain management use cases to learn how your business can benefit from the secure technology.")

![blockchain supply chain use cases and applications](/img/articles/why-and-how-to-employ-blockchain-in-supply-chain-management-tips-and-success-stories/img04.jpg "Discover the globally adopted blockchain supply chain management use cases to learn how your business can benefit from the secure technology.")

There are many companies using blockchain in supply chain operations, and each of them fulfills different needs with the technology. Now, let’s explore the most common blockchain supply chain use cases that represent the above business benefits.

### Provenance Tracking

Blockchain enables accurate tracking of a product’s origin, ensuring authenticity. However, ensuring data accuracy remains critical, as blockchain systems are only as reliable as the data input into them. For example, De Beers uses blockchain to track diamonds from mines to retailers, thus preventing conflict diamonds from entering the market.

In logistics, companies like Maersk use blockchain to monitor the transportation of goods from origin to destination, enhancing traceability. As for manufacturing, companies such as Siemens apply blockchain to track the production process of high-value parts. This way, businesses ensure quality and authenticity throughout the supply chain.

### Counterfeit Prevention

Blockchain in SCM helps verify product authenticity, reducing counterfeit goods. IBM and Walmart, using progressive [logistics and shipping APIs](/blog/a-guide-to-integrating-logistics-and-shipping-apis-to-optimize-your-supply-chain-business/), have implemented blockchain to trace the origin of food products. This way, the companies ensure food safety and prevent contamination.

Retail luxury brands like Louis Vuitton and Prada use blockchain to authenticate high-end goods, guaranteeing that customers receive genuine products. Data accuracy issues are vital here too, as incorrect information on a product’s origin or certification could lead to trust issues with consumers.

In the pharmaceutical industry, leaders like Pfizer leverage blockchain to verify the authenticity of drugs, preventing counterfeit medicines from reaching consumers.

### Inventory Management

Blockchain provides real-time inventory tracking, reducing discrepancies. Maersk and IBM’s TradeLens platform uses blockchain to track shipping containers and [warehousing automation](/blog/chatbots-for-hospitality-and-travel-industries/), improving visibility and reducing delays.

Scalability becomes an issue here as managing the real-time data flow from thousands of containers across a vast network requires a highly scalable solution to avoid bottlenecks.

Getting back to retail, Walmart utilizes blockchain to improve stock management across its stores, ensuring timely restocking and minimizing out-of-stock situations. Manufacturers like Toyota also use blockchain to monitor parts inventory, ensuring parts are available when needed and preventing production delays.

### Automated Payments

Smart contracts on blockchain automate payments upon delivery, reducing administrative overhead. BHP Billiton uses blockchain to streamline supplier payments, ensuring timely and accurate transactions.

FedEx, a logistics company, automates payment processes between suppliers, carriers, and customers for seamless financial exchanges. As a retail giant, Amazon is also exploring blockchain in SCM to automate vendor payments, improving cash flow management and eliminating transactional delays.

### Tokenization of Assets

Tokenization allows the conversion of physical and intangible assets into digital tokens, which can be easily traded and tracked. Real estate companies like Propy use blockchain to tokenize property, enabling buyers and sellers to securely transfer ownership without intermediaries.

Financial companies like Securitize tokenize securities, allowing for fractional ownership and broader market access. In manufacturing, asset-heavy industries such as aerospace can tokenize equipment and machinery, allowing for easier transfer of ownership and streamlined maintenance tracking.

### Smart Contracts in Supply Chain

With smart contracts, you can automate and enforce agreements in the supply chain, ensuring supply chain and blockchain transparency for all parties. As an example, DHL uses blockchain-based contracts to promptly release payments when shipments meet specific delivery milestones.

But, the challenge lies in ensuring that all participants input accurate data, as discrepancies could delay or invalidate contract execution. Thus, in the automotive industry, BMW uses blockchain-based smart contracts to verify the completion of supplier obligations.

### Emphasizing Transparency & Trust

As blockchain builds trust with traceable records of transactions, companies like Nestlé use it to allow consumers to trace the journey of their food products. As a result, you can foster confidence in product quality and sourcing practices.

In logistics, USPS implements blockchain to offer clients full visibility into shipping statuses and transport conditions, strengthening the reputation of their services.

## Challenges and Considerations in Implementing Blockchain in the Supply Chain

![blockchain supply chain challenges and solutions](/img/articles/why-and-how-to-employ-blockchain-in-supply-chain-management-tips-and-success-stories/img05.jpg "When introducing blockchain in the supply chain operations, businesses can face certain challenges, which one can overcome with an agile approach.")

![blockchain supply chain challenges and solutions](/img/articles/why-and-how-to-employ-blockchain-in-supply-chain-management-tips-and-success-stories/img05.jpg "When introducing blockchain in the supply chain operations, businesses can face certain challenges, which one can overcome with an agile approach.")

### Complexity and Fragmentation

Embracing blockchain in supply chain management can be complex due to the fragmented nature of global supply chains, which involve multiple stakeholders, systems, and processes. Integrating blockchain technology across diverse systems and ensuring compatibility can be challenging.

Companies can start with pilot projects before scaling up, establish industry standards and interoperability with [big data](/blog/chatbots-for-hospitality-and-travel-industries/) frameworks, and collaborate with stakeholders to align on common goals and technical requirements to overcome complexity and fragmentation.

### Lack of Transparency

Despite blockchain’s disruptive potential, achieving transparency is challenging due to stakeholder resistance and reluctance to share data openly.

For such issues, you have to build trust by demonstrating transparency benefits like increased efficiency, reduced fraud, and improved traceability. Try adopting incentive mechanisms for data sharing and use permissioned blockchains to allow controlled access to sensitive information, addressing privacy concerns while enhancing transparency.

### Inefficiencies and Delays

Traditional supply chains often face inefficiencies and delays due to manual processes, redundant paperwork, and lack of real-time information. Moreover, employing blockchain for supply chain management requires re-engineering these processes, which can disrupt operations and face resistance.

To deal with it, businesses should conduct thorough process analysis and redesign workflows to leverage blockchain’s strengths, such as [shipping automation](/blog/how-to-automate-the-shipping-process-with-shipping-management-system/) and real-time data sharing. Integrating blockchain with IoT devices and smart contracts can automate data collection and processing, reducing downtimes and improving overall productivity.

### Security Concerns

Although blockchain technology is secure, vulnerabilities like coding errors, poor key management, and insecure interfaces can arise during implementation.

Vendors providing blockchain supply chain solutions follow best practices such as rigorous testing, regular audits, encryption, and secure coding standards. It’s also crucial to operate with multi-layered security measures like multi-signature authentication and secure APIs. Lastly, continuous monitoring and updating can help identify and mitigate security threats promptly.

### Integration with Emerging Technologies

Integrating blockchain can be complex and costly, often requiring extensive upgrades and careful planning to prevent disruptions. It can be an especially troublesome endeavor if you’re operating with legacy software or outdated tools, and the same goes for IoT or AI modules.

Compatibility issues may demand custom solutions, increasing both time and expenses. That is, you need to look for custom solutions, where functionality is focused on dealing with inefficiencies during the transition.

## FAQ

### What are the main benefits of using blockchain in supply chains?

Using blockchain in supply chains ensures enhanced transparency, improved traceability, increased efficiency, reduced fraud, lower costs, and better security through immutable and decentralized data records.

### How does blockchain enhance transparency in supply chain management?

Blockchain promotes accuracy and transparency in supply chain management by providing an immutable and decentralized ledger. It records all transactions and keeps them visible to authorized participants. This ensures that every step of the process is traceable and verifiable.

### How does blockchain enhance security in supply chain management?

Blockchain enhances security in supply chain management by using cryptographic techniques to create immutable and tamper-proof records, ensuring data integrity. Decentralization is designed to minimize the risk of a single point of failure, while smart contracts enforce secure transactions.

### How does blockchain handle data privacy concerns in supply chains?

Blockchain handles data privacy concerns in supply chains by using encryption to protect data, allowing only authorized access. It employs permissioned blockchains to restrict data visibility and ensures that sensitive information is shared securely through smart contracts.

### Is blockchain technology scalable for large supply chains?

Blockchain technology faces scalability challenges for large supply chains. Still, solutions like layer-2 protocols, sharding, and improved consensus algorithms are being developed to enhance capacity and efficiency, making them more suitable for extensive supply chain networks.

Digital Traceability: 5 powerful benefits of blockchain technology for the Department of Defense
================================================================================================

![Image 3: blockchain technology](https://www.siemensgovt.com/assets/img/insights/articles/blockchain.jpg)

The emergence of blockchain technology holds tremendous promise for the U.S. Federal Government to foster trust and greater assurance and security as digitalization advances and threats evolve.

Excitement around the technology is building as federal agencies begin piloting and testing blockchain applications for supply chain and procurement operations, record management, manufacturing, and cybersecurity. At Siemens Government Technologies, we have seen firsthand the powerful benefits blockchain can deliver for the Department of Defense (DoD). While the list of potential blockchain applications continues to grow, here are five compelling benefits within the federal sector today.

What is blockchain?
-------------------

Blockchain is essentially, as the name suggests, a chain of blocks. However, instead of a physical chain, there’s digital information (the block) stored in an unalterable database (the chain). When a block stores new data, it is added to the blockchain and is permanently recorded.

By design, a blockchain is inherently immutable—once recorded, data on the blockchain cannot be changed retroactively without the alteration of all subsequent blocks and a collusion of the network majority. For past records in the blockchain to be altered, all of the users in the network would have to agree on the change and be willing to spend tremendous resources to update all subsequent blocks of the chain. By allowing digital information to be distributed, but not altered without consensus, blockchain technology creates the potential for a new system of trust to be leveraged as the backbone of the internet.

In other words, blockchain technology is essentially “tamper-proof” and one of the most secure data management systems available today.

5 powerful benefits of blockchain technology for the Department of Defense
--------------------------------------------------------------------------

![Image 4: Strengthening cybersecurity](https://www.siemensgovt.com/assets/img/insights/articles/icon-security.jpg)
#### **1.** Strengthening cybersecurity

Blockchain technology can be used to mitigate cybersecurity attacks and to support military operations. Since blockchain is a type of distributed ledger, all network participants share the same documentation, as opposed to individual copies. That shared version can only be updated through consensus, which means everyone must agree on it. To change a single transaction record would require the alteration of all subsequent records and the collusion of the entire network.

Blockchain balances the need for secure data and system visibility. The tamper-proof nature of blockchain makes it difficult to compromise because a successful attack requires the attacker to be able to gain control of many participants. This “cyber-secure” characteristic of blockchain make it an ideal technology for a range of defense applications, such as command and control, control of critical infrastructure, like microgrids, and eliminating the possibility of permanent erasure of data.

![Image 5: supply chain](https://www.siemensgovt.com/assets/img/insights/articles/icon-supply.jpg)
#### **2.** Improving supply chain integrity and traceability

In recent years, the risk of counterfeit, faulty, or malicious components making it into the DoD supply chain has increased dramatically. Blockchain technology acts as a trust enabler within procurement ecosystems by allowing greater visibility and collaboration between origination and destination.

Across the world, companies that depend on their supply chains are turning to blockchain technologies. Manufacturers, retailers, and the transoceanic shipping companies are implementing blockchain-based “track and trace” systems which provide assurance and trust of product components from their origins to the end consumer.

Blockchain technology can bring the defense supply chain into the future by bridging the gap between the physical and digital world and developing a hierarchical logistics ecosystem that is fueled by collaboration and trust. For example, blockchain technology can help secure the DoD’s [microelectronics supply chain](https://www.siemensgovt.com/insights/articles/article-securing-microelectronic-supply-chain.aspx) by improving asset visibility and mitigating risk of counterfeit or fraudulent components.

![Image 6: points of failure](https://www.siemensgovt.com/assets/img/insights/articles/icon-emergency.jpg)
#### **3.** Reducing single points of failure in emergency decision-making

A single point failure in technology is part of a system that, if it fails, will stop the entire system from working. Blockchain technology can reduce single points of failure in emergency and catastrophe decision-making by subjecting decisions to consensus validation through distributed ledger technologies.

For example, in the event of an emergency situation at a military installation, digital solutions can be programmed to use data stored in the blockchain to to root out, isolate, and mitigate against compromises that seek to exploit single points of failure in any networked system. The net result is a command and control hierarchy that can facilitate high confidence in decision making based on the efficacy of data generated from the systems being relied upon.

![Image 7: points of failure](https://www.siemensgovt.com/assets/img/insights/articles/icon-certified.jpg)
#### **4.** Enhancing transparency of procurement auditing

Blockchain technology can improve the integrity, efficiency, and value-for-money of the U.S. Federal Government contract management and procurement processes. Including blockchain technology into the architecture of an e-procurement system comes with the advantage of a ledger that is tamper resistant and capable of being updated and shared in real-time amongst participants in a network to promote trustworthiness and traceability.

The unique ability of blockchain to validate and form consensus around the accuracy and completeness of data being shared on a common ledger amongst a group of semi- to non-trusting parties opens up many possibilities to improve e-procurement for the U.S. government.

Blockchain technology can help the DoD better assess contract award time frames, utilize up-to-the-minute inventory information in procurement decisions, and create an immutable audit trail.

![Image 8: points of failure](https://www.siemensgovt.com/assets/img/insights/articles/icon-cost.jpg)
#### **5.** Reducing costs

A direct benefit of leveraging blockchain technology is that it reduces data reconciliation costs. For the DoD, millions of dollars are spent each year conducting quality assurance checks, data reconciliations, and program audits. The availability of high integrity data through a blockchain allows the DoD to increase their information intelligence through advanced analytics and data science solutions which were previously not possible.

Blockchain can also save money by eliminating unnecessary middlemen who add little, if any, value. Reducing or eliminating manual processes and reducing fraud can all reduce operational costs and further increase efficiency.

For example, a U.S. naval program needed to cut supply chain costs away from inefficient third parties and wanted to lessen the logistical burden. Using blockchain and automated smart contract agreements, the naval program was able to cut the internal order intake processes by 45 percent by taking out manual entering and requiring fewer people to verify yes or no decision making in their logistics process, saving international partners 30 percent in supply chain exchange fees without having to use a third-party system to create the payment.1

Bringing blockchain technology to the U.S. Federal Government
-------------------------------------------------------------

These are just a few of the opportunities for blockchain applications in the government sector. Blockchain solutions are gaining momentum every day. From procurement and supply chain management to energy security and smart infrastructure, blockchain technology can enhance government systems across various platforms and agencies.

# Traceability and Blockchain: Transparency for Any Business

![A professional headshot of Inês Botelho.](/_next/image?url=https%3A%2F%2Fucarecdn.com%2Fa3e8c9a6-914e-49bd-9269-a7cd258592e5%2F&w=128&q=75)
![Cardano Foundation logo on top of an orange gradient, above a globe.](/_next/image?url=https%3A%2F%2Fucarecdn.com%2F1fba840d-68ea-46de-b5d4-8c0dcc2c7e4a%2F&w=3840&q=100)
![Cardano Foundation logo on top of an orange gradient, above a globe.](/_next/image?url=https%3A%2F%2Fucarecdn.com%2F1fba840d-68ea-46de-b5d4-8c0dcc2c7e4a%2F&w=3840&q=100)

An in depth look at the benefits of using blockchain to bolster traceability initiatives

As blockchain technology gains broader acceptance, many seek to understand its advantages as infrastructure. Similar to traditional ledgers, blockchains log transactions or data entries. Yet, they diverge from legacy databases by operating as decentralized ledgers, distributing transaction records across a network of computers—also frequently referred to as nodes—for tamper-proof, immutable storage.

Specifically, blockchain networks function as digital databases where transactions or data entries reside within blocks connected in a chain. Each block holds a unique cryptographic hash referencing earlier blocks, preventing alterations to earlier transactions without disrupting the entire chain. This crucial characteristic safeguards data integrity and security, making the infrastructure ideal for use cases requiring robust traceability.

## What is traceability?

Before delving into blockchain's role in strengthening traceability, let's define the concept. Traceability functions like a product's life story, enabling stakeholders to track its complete journey from creation to end consumer. It involves carefully recording every step the product takes—its origin, the ingredients or materials used, how it was handled and transported, and any changes along the way. These details connect to the product using unique identifiers, akin to how names tie a person to their individual history.

## Why is traceability important?

Traceability provides visibility and accountability throughout the product journey, validating safety, quality, and authenticity. In many ways, it serves as a safety net—if a product proves defective or contaminated, traceability allows for swift identification of the problem's source, minimizing damage. This safeguards consumers and helps companies avoid expensive recalls. It also helps to ensure that products adhere to quality standards and comply with regulations, an essential requirement in industries like food and pharmaceuticals.

Furthermore, traceability promotes trust among businesses within a supply chain, helping to streamline processes and eliminate inefficiencies by ensuring everyone has access to the same information. Businesses can also boost customer loyalty and product interest through increased transparency and brand relatability powered by traceability solutions. Notably, a growing number of consumers demand transparency as it empowers them to understand a product's origins, production methods, and alignment with environmental, humanitarian, and personal values. These insights foster informed decision-making and strengthen brand loyalty.

## How does blockchain traceability work?

The [immutability and transparency of blockchain](https://cardanofoundation.org/blog/blockchain-basics-simple-guide-beginners) make it an ideal technology for tracking and verifying the origins and movement of goods or data through supply chains. On a more granular level, blockchain traceability works like a highly secure digital passport or stock keeping unit (SKU) for an item moving through a supply chain. Each step of the item's journey gets recorded in a unique information or data unit. The data units are then grouped together in blocks, which appear linked chronologically on a shared digital ledger—the blockchain. This ledger becomes practically unchangeable, and only those with authorized access can see it. Permissionless blockchains, however, take traceability and transparency one step further and make all transactions public. In permissioned blockchains, only a select group of participants can access the network, maintaining a degree of privacy. Conversely, anyone can view the transactions on permissionless blockchains, ensuring full transparency and promoting trustlessness.

Every change, like a new location, temperature reading, or transfer of ownership, is added to a block of transactions. This data generates a complete, immutable history. Blockchain also complements emerging technologies like AI and synthetic biology, both novel tools that benefit from verifiable infrastructure and credentials. As global leaders aim to maintain the integrity of global systems amidst such technological advances, transparency is more important than ever—especially in commerce, international trade, and governance. Such an undertaking needs openness, accountability, and flexible yet robust infrastructure. Cardano's potential becomes apparent considering these requirements.

## Leveraging traceability on Cardano

Cardano already supports decentralized applications (DApps) across diverse use cases, with a resilient infrastructure designed for transparency. Businesses can specifically leverage [Cardano's advanced features](https://cardanofoundation.org/blog/introduction-cardano-blockchain) to bolster traceability initiatives, unlocking benefits such as:

## The Cardano Foundation’s traceability initiatives

The Cardano Foundation pursues traceability solutions that promote transparency, accountability, and trust across diverse use cases. With its secure and immutable ledger, the Cardano blockchain provides a suitable framework for recording and verifying the movement of goods and assets throughout their lifecycle. This enables enhanced supply chain management, product authentication, and counterfeit prevention, reinforcing customer engagement and brand reputation.

## Traceability from vine to glass

The Foundation collaborated with the wine industry in the [Republic of Georgia](https://cardanofoundation.org/blog/cardano-foundation-partners-with-georgian-national-wine-agency), home of the world's oldest winemaking heritage. The [Baia's Wine and Scantrust](https://cardanofoundation.org/blog/cardano-reveals-its-first-supply-chain-solution-in-association-with-scantrust) project initially tackled the challenges of counterfeiting and heavily paper-based export processes by digitizing procedures and linking them with digital signatures shared on the blockchain.

This decentralized approach means no single authority controls records, making the system resistant to censorship, tampering, and single points of failure. Furthermore, entries can be validated even if participants in the international supply chain access the information via different applications. Notably, the initiative to safeguard the authenticity of Baia's Wine through digital signatures resulted in an increase in sales across different international markets. Expanding the initiative with the National Wine Agency of Georgia, the Bolnisi Project today encompasses over 30 wineries in the Bolnisi region, preserving Georgia's rich winemaking legacy.

## Advantages of tokenizing physical goods

Similarly, the limited edition [proof-of-concept (POC) Hoodie](https://store.cardano.org/products/hoodie) from the [Cardano Store](https://store.cardano.org/) reinforces [traceability principles](https://store.cardano.org/pages/authenticated-products) by tokenizing each sweater using an NFC chip connected to a verification link. When scanned, the NFC retrieves the immutable blockchain record, authenticating the hoodie. Further highlighting traceability efforts, the Foundation had already [partnered with veritree](https://cardanofoundation.org/blog/cardano-unveils-multiple-strategic-partnerships-at-the-cardano-summit-2021) during the Cardano Summit 2021 to establish the Cardano Forest. This collaboration involved the community sponsoring the planting of one million trees, connected to token records on Cardano's blockchain for transparent ownership verification and environmental impact tracking.

## Track and Trace with Cardano products

The Cardano Explorer also enhances accessibility and traceability, allowing anyone to view the history of on-chain transactions. The Explorer offers enhanced visualization of multiple components, promoting a better understanding of Cardano’s design decisions and on-chain transactions. Similarly, the Foundation’s Engineering Team has started to develop a Java-based data provisioning tool called [Ledger Sync](https://cardanofoundation.org/blog/accessing-cardano-blockchain-data-with-ledger-sync), which simplifies access to Cardano blockchain data via a widely used programming language. With this easier access to data, users can trace network activity with more clarity and precision.

Such initiatives demonstrate blockchain technology's immense potential for enhancing traceability and transparency across diverse businesses. Companies can embrace blockchain solutions to improve operational efficiency, build stakeholder trust, and foster greater customer engagement and satisfaction. Using Cardano infrastructure, businesses can easily navigate the complexities of blockchain integration, unlocking the benefits of traceability solutions for a brighter future.

Another way to strengthen supply chain operations would be to mark inventory with either RFID tags or electronic product codes that adhere to GS1 standards (globally accepted rules for handling supply chain data) and to then integrate a company’s ERP systems with those of its suppliers to construct a complete record of transactions. Part of the appeal of using blockchain to enhance supply chain efficiency and speed is that these applications, much like those for improving traceability, require participating companies to share only limited data—in this case, just inventory or shipment data.
